title: (Rootless) after a container in a network exits, all other containers in the network lose connectivity
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind bug

**Description**

On rootless podman, when a container exits, all other containers in the network lose connectivity.

New containers created have connectivity, until another container exits.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Install [Fedora 37: Standard ISO image for x86_64](https://getfedora.org/en/server/download/).
    - Problem originally noticed on a "native install".
    - Problem reproduced in a qemu VM.
    - Problem also reproduced on a Fedora 37 server on a cloud host (Digital Ocean).
2. Upgrade system and install podman.
3. As an unprivileged user:
    - `podman network create tmp-network`
    - `podman run --name debug1 --network tmp-network --rm -it alpine:3.17 sh`
    - In container `debug1`, run `ping 1.1.1.1` or `wget -S google.com` - it works.
    - In a new window: `podman run --name debug2 --network tmp-network --rm -it alpine:3.17 sh -c 'echo another container exits'`

At this point, an existing ping process works, but any new connections don't (e.g. if you ctrl-c the old ping process and run ping again, it hangs).

**Describe the results you received:**

```
64 bytes from 1.1.1.1: seq=20 ttl=42 time=2.283 ms
64 bytes from 1.1.1.1: seq=21 ttl=42 time=2.248 ms
64 bytes from 1.1.1.1: seq=22 ttl=42 time=3.863 ms
64 bytes from 1.1.1.1: seq=23 ttl=42 time=2.238 ms
64 bytes from 1.1.1.1: seq=24 ttl=42 time=2.305 ms
^C
--- 1.1.1.1 ping statistics ---
25 packets transmitted, 25 packets received, 0% packet loss
round-trip min/avg/max = 2.135/2.442/3.863 ms
/ # echo 'previous ping process continued working, but nothing new can connect to the outside world'
previous ping process continued working, but nothing new can connect to the outside world
/ # ping 1.1.1.1
PING 1.1.1.1 (1.1.1.1): 56 data bytes
^C
--- 1.1.1.1 ping statistics ---
8 packets transmitted, 0 packets received, 100% packet loss
/ # wget -S google.com
Connecting to google.com (142.251.32.78:80)
^C
/ # 
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

`ping` and `wget` and anything that connects to the internet (e.g. also tested: `ncat` cannot reach external hosts (after a container in the network exits)) can continue to connect to the internet after another container in the network exits.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

Issue is always reproducible. I am using output from Fedora 37 in a VM I just freshly created.

Logs from journalctl - the lines `podman-test kernel: podman1: port 2(veth1) entered disabled state` after `another container exits` seem suspicious to me?


- qemu VM

comments.body: If you start another container does it work again? This sounds like https://github.com/containers/netavark/issues/491

Hi, thanks for the quick response. I just tested: if I start another container (that doesn't exit), networking does work again in the original container after about 10s (not sure what's happening in the 10s between container start and connectivity being restored, but the behaviour is consistent). I see that https://github.com/containers/netavark/pull/494 is merged, which is in the 1.4.0 release 3 days ago.

If I want to confirm/use the fix, besides waiting for netavark 1.4.0 to hit the official repositories, is the only other/best way to compile it from source and install as root?

Thank you again!
You can try it from the podman-next copr repo, https://copr.fedorainfracloud.org/coprs/rhcontainerbot/podman-next/

I added the repo and ran `sudo dnf install --repo copr:copr.fedorainfracloud.org:rhcontainerbot:podman-next netavark`, but it just tells me

> Package netavark-1.3.0-1.fc37.x86_64 is already installed.

I ended up just manually replacing `/usr/libexec/podman/netavark` with the the [1.4.0 release from github](https://github.com/containers/netavark/releases/tag/v1.4.0). Presumably, I could uninstall podman and rerun the above `dnf` command, but I wasn't sure if weird things would happen to my existing containers. Getting a bit off topic, but what would have been the proper thing to do?

In any case, netavark 1.4.0 seems to have fixed the issue. Much thanks!

I am not a packager so I am not sure how this really works but I think install will always say the package is already installed. You could try with `dnf update ....`

Glad it is working, the new version should get into the official fedora repo soon.
